corrections:
## Corrections to the researcher's survey (verified 2026-07-15)

### 1. Parakeet-tdt-0.6b-v3 is NOT "cache-aware streaming" — and its default streaming latency is seconds, not sub-second
The model card (https://huggingface.co/nvidia/parakeet-tdt-0.6b-v3) never uses the phrase "cache-aware" in its streaming section. The referenced script is `NeMo/examples/asr/asr_chunked_inference/rnnt/speech_to_text_streaming_infer_rnnt.py` — **chunked/buffered streaming inference** over an offline-trained model, with `chunk_secs=2, right_context_secs=2.0, left_context_secs=10.0`. NVIDIA's genuinely cache-aware streaming FastConformer models (e.g. `stt_en_fastconformer_hybrid_large_streaming_multi`) are a **separate, English-only** family — not available for Dutch. Practical consequence the survey glosses over: with the documented defaults, algorithmic latency is roughly chunk + right-context ≈ **4 s** before a token can finalize; you can tune both down at an accuracy cost, but "genuine low-latency streaming" overstates what the card documents. It is still a reasonable top pick, but the latency claim should be validated empirically against Voxtral-Realtime (which is architecturally streaming at 80–480 ms delays).

### 2. vLLM on DGX Spark no longer "requires a custom build" — the survey's biggest outdated conclusion
Issue vllm-project/vllm#36821 is indeed still open (checked via GitHub API 2026-07-15), and stable prebuilt wheels/images still stop at sm_120. But since the survey's March-2026 sources:
- The **official vLLM blog post "vLLM on the DGX Spark"** (https://vllm.ai/blog/2026-06-01-vllm-dgx-spark, 2026-06-01) documents running vLLM on GB10 using the prebuilt **`vllm/vllm-openai:cu130-nightly`** image (their advice: pin a validated digest for deployment).
- **NVIDIA publishes an official vLLM playbook for Spark** (https://build.nvidia.com/spark/vllm) and NGC vLLM containers (`nvcr.io/nvidia/vllm`, e.g. 26.02-py3) support GB10.
- Community images/one-command setups exist (e.g. github.com/eelbaz/dgx-spark-vllm-setup, timothystewart6/vllm-gb10).
- The earlier tracking issue #31128 "[Feature]: Add support of Blackwell SM121 (DGX Spark)" was closed **completed** on 2025-12-23.

Net effect: the "highest deployment risk" rating on Voxtral-Mini-4B-Realtime and qwen3-vllm is overstated as of mid-2026. The vLLM path on Spark is: use the cu130-nightly/NGC image — no custom compile needed. The Transformers ≥5.2.0 fallback claim remains correct.

### 3. faster-whisper issue #1431 is CLOSED (but the underlying problem persists)
SYSTRAN/faster-whisper#1431 "Support CUDA 13" was closed 2026-03-29 — however it was closed only with "Please post this issue on CTranslate2 Repo", not fixed. The live issue is **OpenNMT/CTranslate2#1933 "Feature request: CUDA 13 support" (still open)**, and the latest CTranslate2 release **v4.8.1 (2026-07-03)** contains no CUDA 13 support. So the survey's substance (CUDA-12 cuBLAS/cuDNN needed alongside CUDA 13, or use the whisperx-blackwell route) stands; only the issue reference/status is stale.

### 4. PyTorch-on-sm_121 guidance is wrong: it's cu130 (or NGC), not "cu128/cu129"
Official **PyTorch cu130 aarch64 wheels** exist and are the recommended pip path for GB10 (`pip install torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu130`); sm_120 kernels are binary-compatible with sm_121 per PyTorch maintainers, and NGC `pytorch:25.10-py3`+ (NVRTC 13.0, natively knows sm_121) is the cleanest container path. "cu128" appears only as a nightly+NVRTC-symlink workaround; "cu129" is not part of any current guidance. Sources: https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/dgx-spark-gb10-cuda-13-0-python-3-12-sm-121/223744, https://github.com/natolambert/dgx-spark-setup, https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/nvidia-dgx-spark-support/223677.

### 5. WhisperLiveKit: "simulstreaming" is a policy, not a backend
Current README: `--backend` accepts {auto, faster-whisper, whisper, openai-api, voxtral, voxtral-mlx, mlx-whisper, qwen3-vllm, qwen3-vllm-metal, qwen3-streaming}; **simulstreaming and localagreement are `--backend-policy` values** applied over Whisper-family backends. Functionally the survey's point holds (SimulStreaming-style streaming is available), but configuration examples written as `--backend simulstreaming` would fail.

### 6. Voxtral-Realtime delay range is discrete, not continuous
The card specifies delays of **any multiple of 80 ms between 80 and 1200 ms, plus 2400 ms as a standalone value** (headline: "configurable transcription delays (240ms to 2.4s)"). "Configurable 80 ms–2.4 s" is right at the endpoints but there is a gap between 1.2 s and 2.4 s.

### 7. yuriyvnv 4.43% claim — confirmed, but with a red flag the survey missed
4.43% is this exact model's CV17-nl test WER, **but the same card reports 20.29% WER on MLS Dutch** — dramatically worse than vanilla multilingual models (canary-1b-v2: 11.27% MLS nl; parakeet-v3: 12.78%). This is hard evidence for the survey's own caveat: this fine-tune overfits Common Voice read speech and is likely a poor choice for spontaneous/streaming audio.

### 8. Jaspernl/whisper-large-v3-ft-nl is not publicly accessible
HF API returns **HTTP 401** (private/gated-inaccessible) as of 2026-07-15. Its claimed datasets (CV13+VoxPopuli) and any WER are unverifiable; it should not be listed as an available option without that caveat.

### 9. SimulStreaming license is now plain MIT
GitHub's license detection for ufal/SimulStreaming reports **MIT** (2026-07-15). The survey's hedge about "dual-use/research clauses noted historically" is now moot — the earlier PolyForm-noncommercial/commercial dual licensing is gone.

### 10. Minor unverified figures (flagged, not refuted)
- canary-1b-v2 "avg ~8.1% beating whisper-large-v3 ~9.9%": the card's aggregate rows I could verify were "Fleurs-25 Langs 8.40%" and "MLS-6 Langs 7.27%"; the 8.1/9.9 cross-benchmark averages were not directly confirmed.
- Kyutai "400 streams/H100" and whisper.cpp performance ranking on GB10: not checked against primary sources.
- Qwen3-ASR timestamps come via the **separate companion model Qwen3-ForcedAligner-0.6B**, not the ASR checkpoints themselves; the paper counts "52 languages and dialects" (30 languages + 22 Chinese dialects).
- The Open ASR Leaderboard Space UI (Dutch filter widget) could not be rendered (JS-only); tracks and datasets (fleurs/mcv/mls) were confirmed from the paper and the GitHub repo instead. Note the repo README (updated 24 June 2026) says multilingual/long-form evals "will migrate to HF Jobs in the future", i.e. the multilingual track is refreshed less rigorously than the English track.

corrections:
All twelve load-bearing claims are CONFIRMED against primary sources. The survey is accurate and current for mid-2026. A few minor items to flag:

## Minor corrections / caveats

- **CALLHOME column mislabel (Sortformer v2 table).** The survey's comparison lists a "CALLHOME-part2 2spk DER" column with values 5.34 / 6.05 / 6.57 / 6.91 %. The actual v2 model-card README column I fetched is labeled **"CALLHOME-part2 Full DER"** with values **9.54 / 10.15 / 10.70 / 11.38 %**. The card may contain a separate 2-speaker subset column that produces the lower figures, but those exact numbers were not surfaced and should be re-checked before quoting. The DIHARD III ≤4spk numbers (13.45 / 13.75 / 13.24 / 13.44 %) and RTF span (0.002–0.180) are exact and correct.

- **v2.1 meeting-domain DER improvement not independently verified.** Existence, ungated status, NVIDIA Open Model License ('other'), and ~105k downloads (104,835) are confirmed. The claim that v2.1 "substantially improves meeting-domain DER (AMI / AliMeeting / NOTSOFAR-1) over v2" was not directly read from the v2.1 card's tables in this pass — treat as plausible-but-unverified. The survey itself notes v2.1's DIHARD numbers are not directly comparable to v2's, which is a fair caveat.

- **PyTorch cu130 aarch64 currency.** torch 2.9.1+cu130 aarch64 (cp310–cp314) wheels do exist and are installable on Python 3.12. However, as of 2026-07 they are **no longer the latest**: download.pytorch.org/whl/cu130/torch/ now serves up to **torch 2.13.0** (present: 2.9.0, 2.9.1, 2.10.0, 2.11.0, 2.12.0, 2.12.1, 2.13.0). This does not affect installability (WhisperLiveKit's diart extra still pins torch<2.9, so the sortformer path with a newer torch remains the clean route), but the plan pinning "torch 2.9.1+cu130" is now a deliberately older choice rather than the newest available. The claimed 2026-01-26 upload date was not independently confirmed.

- **diart / Sortformer paper DER figures are paper-sourced.** The ~28–30 % DIHARD III DER for diart is from the paper's results table and was not re-derived; the load-bearing packaging facts (versions, dates, licenses, pyannote<3.1 recommendation, gated deps) are all confirmed.

- **sherpa-onnx version note (non-load-bearing).** PyPI metadata surfaced 1.13.4 as latest with an early-2025 date; the repo is very actively pushed, so newer point releases likely exist. This does not affect the offline-only conclusion, which is confirmed by source-file presence (offline headers 200, online headers 404).

No claim was refuted. The overall recommendation stack (Streaming Sortformer v2/v2.1 primary, WhisperLiveKit integration, diart fallback for >4 speakers, pyannote community-1 offline reference) is well-supported by primary evidence.

verdicts.claim: Claim 7: WhisperLiveKit attributes words to speakers by max temporal overlap between punctuation-delimited token groups and diarization segments (intersection_duration/get_lines_diarization), buffering unlabeled tokens when diarization lags
verdicts.evidence: tokens_alignment.py contains intersection_duration (max(0,end-start) overlap), compute_punctuations_segments (groups tokens by punctuation/silence into PuncSegment), concatenate_diar_segments (merges consecutive same-speaker segments), and get_lines_diarization implementing argmax-overlap assignment (max_overlap_speaker = diarization_segment.speaker + 1). corrections:
## Corrections and material nuances to the survey

### 1. speaches DOES have speaker diarization (survey §4 and claim 9 are wrong on this)
speaches added a pyannote-based **speaker diarization REST endpoint** on 2025-12-01 (commit "feat: speaker diarization endpoint"), shipped in the v0.9.0-rc.3 era. Current master contains `src/speaches/diarization.py`, `src/speaches/executors/pyannote_diarization.py`, `src/speaches/executors/wespeaker_speaker_embedding.py`, `src/speaches/routers/diarization.py`, and `tests/diarization_test.py`; it returns speaker-labeled segments and supports known-speaker reference matching. Active issues (#661 2026-07-09, #629 memory leak) confirm it is a live feature. It appears to be **batch/upload diarization, not streaming**, so speaches is still not a complete realtime solution — but "no diarization at all" is factually wrong. Source: https://github.com/speaches-ai/speaches (tree + commit history).

### 2. WLK's benchmark already includes Dutch data out of the box
The survey says the benchmark "uses LibriVox+Gutenberg; same pattern works for Dutch LibriVox" and Dutch samples must be added. In fact `whisperlivekit/benchmark/datasets.py` already catalogs **Multilingual LibriSpeech Dutch** (config `"dutch"`, `language: "nl"`) alongside en/fr/es/de/pt/it/pl — `wlk bench --languages all` covers Dutch WER today. Caveat: only **1 Dutch sample** (`n_samples: 1`), so a real Dutch eval set is still needed, and there is still no DER. Source: https://github.com/QuentinFuxa/WhisperLiveKit/blob/main/whisperlivekit/benchmark/datasets.py

### 3. Voxtral-HF and Sortformer diarization are DECLARED-CONFLICTING extras in WLK — this breaks the survey's proposed experiment matrix
WLK's README (line ~141) states: "`voxtral-hf` ... and `diarization-sortformer` are also intentionally incompatible extras and must be installed in separate environments", and `pyproject.toml` lists `{extra = "voxtral-hf"}` vs `{extra = "diarization-sortformer"}` in its conflicts table (likely because `nemo-toolkit[asr]` pins `transformers~=4.57.0`). The survey's recommended A/B plan ("whisper vs Voxtral-Mini-4B-Realtime, Sortformer vs diart") implies one environment doing Voxtral **with** Sortformer diarization — per upstream that combination is unsupported in a single env. Plan for two venvs or verify the conflict can be worked around. Source: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/QuentinFuxa/WhisperLiveKit/main/pyproject.toml

### 4. The diart extra pins torch<2.9.0 — a direct conflict with DGX Spark's GPU requirements
`diarization-diart` extra = `diart, torch<2.9.0, torchaudio<2.9.0, torchvision<0.24.0`, and it is also declared conflicting with the `cu129` extra. DGX Spark (sm_121, CUDA 13) needs a recent cu13 torch (the survey itself says to install torch 2.13.0/cu13); torch<2.9 will not have working sm_121+cu13 aarch64 wheels. So "benchmark diart vs Sortformer for Dutch" is materially harder on this machine than the survey implies — diart may have to run CPU-only or with a manually overridden torch pin.

### 5. Sortformer extra is Python <3.13 only, and not pinned to nemo 2.7.3
The extra is `nemo-toolkit[asr]>2.4; python_version >= '3.10' and python_version < '3.13'` — WLK's headline "3.11–3.13" support does not extend to Sortformer diarization on 3.13 (irrelevant here on 3.12.3, but worth knowing). Also nemo 2.7.3 is just the current latest satisfying `>2.4`, not a requirement.

### 6. CTranslate2 aarch64 wheels: stronger than the survey states — they contain NO CUDA at all
The survey says prebuilt CUDA support "for sm_121 is not there" / "CT2 targets CUDA ≤12.8". Primary evidence is more absolute: CT2's official wheel-build script (`python/tools/prepare_build_environment_linux.sh`) builds **aarch64 wheels CPU-only** (OpenBLAS/Ruy, no `-DWITH_CUDA`); only x86_64 wheels are built `-DWITH_CUDA=ON` against CUDA 12.8. So on this machine `pip install ctranslate2` gives zero GPU support for ANY compute capability, and faster-whisper falls back to CPU. CUDA 13 support is an open feature request (OpenNMT/CTranslate2#1933, 2025-11-17, still open). This reinforces the recommendation to use the pure-PyTorch SimulStreaming path / Voxtral. Note also: whisperx-blackwell's README headline hack is a PyTorch `get_device_capability()` spoof (12,1)→(9,0); the CT2 v4.7.1 source build lives in its `Dockerfile.gpu`, and the project targets batch WhisperX, was last pushed 2026-04-23, default branch is `master` (not `main`).

### 7. "CT2 disables INT8 on sm_120 Blackwell" — unverified
I found no primary evidence for this specific statement in CT2 release notes or docs during this pass; treat it as unconfirmed until sourced.

### 8. Minor metadata corrections
- "8 open issues" on WhisperLiveKit = GitHub `open_issues_count`, which includes PRs; the true split is **7 issues + 1 PR** (still exceptionally healthy).
- whisperlivekit 0.2.24's **PyPI classifier still says "License :: OSI Approved :: MIT License"** while the bundled license text and GitHub say Apache-2.0 — a packaging-metadata leftover worth noting for any compliance review (the LICENSE file governs).
- Hyprnote is now branded **anarlog** (the team's commercial effort moved to "char"); its release tag format is `desktop_v1.1.16` (2026-07-14) — consistent with "desktop-only".
- RealtimeSTT stars are 9,986 ("~10k" fine); it was last pushed 2026-06-12 — moderately, not intensely, active.
- Vexa's latest release is **v0.12.4 (2026-07-14)** — the survey gave no version; otherwise its characterization (meeting-bot API, Apache-2.0, 2.5k stars) is accurate.
- Vibe's README does advertise a "Realtime preview" feature — this is live preview during batch file transcription, not live-microphone streaming, so the survey's classification stands but the wording "not streaming" could be misread; Vibe is Tauri desktop, MIT, v3.0.22 (2026-07-12), with diarization, as stated.

### 9. Things I could NOT verify on-machine (installs were out of scope for this verification)
- `torch.cuda.get_arch_list()` including sm_121 for torch 2.13.0 cu13 aarch64 wheels — wheel existence and nvidia-*-cu13 deps confirmed on PyPI; actual GB10 kernel coverage still needs the survey's own proposed on-machine test after install.
- End-to-end pip resolution of `nemo_toolkit[asr]==2.7.3` on aarch64/py3.12 — the 42-dep `[asr]` extra looks mostly pure-Python (lhotse, lightning<=2.4.0, transformers~=4.57.0, pyannote.core/metrics, kaldi-python-io, sox<=1.5.0), but this remains untested, as the survey itself flags.
- Issue #276's exact "ptxas fatal: 'sm_121a' not defined" quote sits in the issue thread beyond the excerpt I fetched; the sm_121a title, torch 2.9.1+cu130 detail, DGX Spark context, and closed status are all directly confirmed.

### Bottom line
The survey is accurate on all load-bearing WhisperLiveKit facts (license chain incl. the SimulStreaming MIT relicense of 2025-10-22, versions, endpoints, benchmark harness, DGX Spark issues) and on the competitive landscape, with one outright factual error (speaches diarization), one omission that helps us (Dutch already in `wlk bench` datasets), and two environment-planning gotchas that matter for the recommended plan on this specific machine (voxtral-hf × diarization-sortformer extras conflict; diart extra's torch<2.9 pin vs the cu13/sm_121 torch requirement)."

corrections:
## Corrections and refinements to the survey

All 12 load-bearing claims survived verification; the survey is overall accurate and current. The following details are wrong, outdated, or need sharpening:

### 1. nvidia-smi field wording (claim 11 / gotcha 2) — minor but locally refuted as literally stated
On this exact machine (driver 580.159.03), the Memory-Usage column reads **"Not Supported"**, not "N/A" (verified by running `nvidia-smi` on the target). The natolambert README's "N/A" phrasing likely reflects an older driver or paraphrase. Substance is unchanged (no usable total-memory reading). Additionally, the survey's "no reliable per-process GPU memory" is overstated: the nvidia-smi process table DOES print per-process figures on this box (Xorg 43MiB, gnome-shell 16MiB, python3 170MiB) — though these may undercount unified-memory allocations, so `free -h` / `torch.cuda.memory_allocated()` remains the right advice.

### 2. whisper.cpp version and prebuilt binaries (section c) — outdated
- Current whisper.cpp release is **v1.9.1 (2026-06-19)**, not v1.8.4 (v1.8.5 2026-05-29, v1.8.6 2026-06-02, v1.8.7 2026-06-16, v1.9.0 2026-06-17 in between). Source: api.github.com/repos/ggml-org/whisper.cpp/releases. The forum report used v1.8.4 because it was current on 2026-05-30.
- "No prebuilt ARM64+CUDA binaries exist" is now only half-true: **since v1.8.7 (2026-06-16), releases include a prebuilt `whisper-bin-ubuntu-arm64.tar.gz`** — but it is CPU-only (the only CUDA release assets remain `whisper-cublas-*-bin-x64.zip`, x86_64/Windows). Conclusion unchanged — a source build is still required for GPU on GB10 — but the flat statement is outdated.

### 3. `pip install nemo-toolkit-asr` will not work as written (section d / claim 12) — actionable gap
Every release of `nemo-toolkit-asr` on PyPI is a **pre-release** (2.7.0rc0 … 2.8.0rc2; no stable release exists). A plain `pip install nemo-toolkit-asr` resolves to nothing — it needs `--pre` (or an explicit `==2.8.0rc2` pin). Also note the official docs' own pip fallback is the **classic package with extras** — `pip install 'nemo-toolkit[asr,tts,cu13]' --extra-index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu132` (note: NVIDIA's docs point at **cu132**, not cu130) — not `nemo-toolkit-asr`. Source: https://pypi.org/pypi/nemo-toolkit-asr/json and https://docs.nvidia.com/nemo/speech/nightly/starthere/install.html.

### 4. torchcodec pairing table (section a / claim 9) — slightly incomplete
The torchcodec README table shows **0.12, 0.13, and 0.14 all pair with torch>=2.11** (Python >=3.10,<=3.14) — not just 0.13/0.14. Also, `torchcodec-0.15.0+cu130` cp312 aarch64 wheels exist on the cu130 index (newer than the README table row set); with torch pinned to 2.11.0 you should pin torchcodec `<=0.14.0` explicitly (the survey's `torchcodec==0.14.0` pin in the setup script is correct; the unpinned `torchcodec` in the earlier "Recommended install" snippet could pull 0.15.0, whose torch requirement is undocumented in the table fetched today). FFmpeg 4-8 support confirmed from the README ("TorchCodec supports all major FFmpeg versions in [4, 8]").

### 5. assix CTranslate2 binaries were built against Python 3.10.14 (section b / claim 5)
The repo's prebuilt archive is C++ libs + headers built on a Spark with **Python 3.10.14**, Ubuntu 24.04.3, CUDA 13.0, cuDNN 9.14.0, GCC 13.3.0. The survey's plan (build the Python wrapper locally from `python/` against the py3.12 venv) handles this correctly, but anyone tempted to reuse any Python artifacts from that repo on py3.12 should not. Also worth restating the survey's own caveat: this is one community data point for CT2 **4.6.0**; 4.8.x + CUDA 13 on Spark is unconfirmed anywhere I could find.

### 6. Small confirmations that strengthen (not weaken) the survey
- cu132 index actually carries torch 2.12.0, **2.12.1**, and 2.13.0 aarch64 cp312 (survey said "2.12.0–2.13.0" — fine).
- cu131 and cu133 confirmed 403 from this machine.
- pypi.nvidia.com/ctranslate2/ confirmed 404 (NoSuchKey).
- torchaudio cu130 **x86_64** also tops out at 2.11.0 — supports the maintenance-mode framing (this is a project-wide stop, not an aarch64 gap).
- torchaudio 2.11.0 wheel metadata confirmed to have no torch pin (requires_dist is null), exactly as the survey noted.
- jetson-ai-lab sbsa/cu130 index confirmed to host torch 2.9.0/2.9.1/2.10.0/2.11.0 and flash_attn 2.8.4 linux_aarch64 alongside onnxruntime_gpu 1.24.0.
- faster-whisper 1.2.1 deps confirmed exactly as listed (ctranslate2<5,>=4.0; onnxruntime<2,>=1.14; av>=11; tokenizers; huggingface-hub; tqdm).
- NGC sizes/dates match to the day: nemo:26.06 arm64 18.2 GB created 2026-06-22; pytorch:26.06-py3 arm64 10.9 GB created 2026-06-26.

### 7. Things I could not verify (unchanged uncertainty, flagged so they aren't read as verified)
- The actual runtime behavior checks that require installation (torch import/matmul on sm_121, CT2 rebuild, whisper.cpp dual-arch build test, nemo import on aarch64) were out of scope for this read-only pass; those claims rest on the primary forum/repo reports cited above, all of which I fetched and which say what the survey says they say.
- The claim inside the forum thread that nvcr.io CUDA images lacked ARM64 tags (May 2026) remains unverified, as the survey itself flagged.
- The rappdw/transcribe-dgx, atripathy86/transcribe, Mekopa/whisperx-blackwell prior-art repos were not individually re-verified (non-load-bearing pointers)."

corrections:
## Corrections and cautions for the survey

### 1. Section 9 (Evaluation tooling) is out of date on meeteval / DER — the biggest error
The survey states: *"meeteval focuses on WER variants; compute DER with pyannote.metrics, dscore (md-eval), or spyder"* and Claim 9 asserts DER is done *"rather than meeteval."* This is now wrong. The current meeteval README (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/fgnt/meeteval/main/README.md) explicitly lists **"Diarization Error Rate (DER)"** as a supported metric, implemented by **wrapping md-eval (md-eval-22.pl) "like dscore."** meeteval also now ships tcpWER, tcORC-WER, tcMIMO-WER, greedy tcORC-WER, and DI-cpWER. So meeteval can serve as both the speaker-attributed-WER **and** the DER engine (its DER is just an md-eval wrapper, so numbers should match dscore). pyannote.metrics/dscore remain valid alternatives, but the framing that meeteval cannot do DER is incorrect.

### 2. IFADV Zenodo "re-published 2025-09-16" is not what the record shows
The survey says the Zenodo record was "re-published 2025-09-16." The record page (https://zenodo.org/records/14906857) reports the publication date as **28 May 2008 (Version v1)**. The record may carry a later Zenodo upload/update timestamp, but the stated publication metadata is 2008, not 2025-09-16 — treat the 2025 date as unverified. (Liveness/HTTP 200 and downloadability today are confirmed.)

### 3. IFADV recording vs. annotation counts — keep them distinct
Both primary sources agree the **annotated** set is **20 dialogues x 15 min = 5 h**. The Zenodo description additionally notes that **24** dialogues (6 h) were originally recorded, of which 20 were fully annotated. The survey's "~34 unique speakers (Zenodo) vs 40 speaker-slots (UvA)" reconciliation is correct: Zenodo says "34 speakers ... 10 male and 24 female"; UvA counts 40 participant slots across 20 dyads.

### 4. Common Voice 17.0 gating claim is unconfirmed
The survey says the older `mozilla-foundation/common_voice_17_0` "still on HF but requires accepting terms / login." The fetched page prominently shows the Oct-2025 Mozilla Data Collective migration notice and its file listing appeared empty; it did **not** clearly present a login/terms gate in this fetch. The migration notice is confirmed; the residual gating/availability of the 17.0 files on HF should be re-checked before relying on that route.

### 5. LibriMix "DutchMix" is not turnkey
LibriMix's documented inputs are LibriSpeech + WHAM noise; the README does **not** document custom/Dutch source data or native RTTM output. Synthesizing Dutch mixtures with exact reference RTTM is achievable but requires adapting the metadata CSVs and generating RTTM yourself from the per-source signals — present it as an integration task, not an out-of-the-box script. SparseLibriMix's specific claims were not independently re-verified this pass.

### 6. Minor: FLEURS license precision
FLEURS is fine as stated (CC-BY, nl_nl, ~1509/150/350). Note the exact HF card license tag is typically `cc-by-4.0`; the split figures were confirmed via the FLEURS paper (arXiv 2205.12446) because the live HF card body was truncated on direct fetch.

### 7. Confirmed as-is (no correction needed)
IFADV stereo-channel layout, MLS Dutch stats/license/speaker_id, CGN "free but signed license / comp-a ~99.5h face-to-face," fsicoli CC0 nl mirror, VoxPopuli nl (53h, 221 speakers, per-speaker paragraphs -> <=20s), JASMIN academic-license access, and N-Best 2008 ELRA-only distribution all checked out against primary sources.

It also lists tcpWER, tcORC WER, tcMIMO WER, greedy variants, and DI-cpWER. So meeteval DOES compute DER as of 2026-07.

verdicts.claim: LibriMix (JorisCos/LibriMix) and SparseLibriMix provide scripts to synthesize 2/3-speaker mixtures with per-source signals, allowing exact reference RTTM+transcripts to be built from Dutch single-speaker data.
verdicts.evidence: https://github.com/JorisCos/LibriMix README confirms generate_librimix.sh: 'LibriMix will be generated for 2 and 3 speakers, at both 16Khz and 8kHz', modes min/max, mix_clean/mix_both/mix_single, and n_src configurable; per-source signals are produced (basis for deriving RTTM/transcripts). HOWEVER the README does NOT document custom (non-LibriSpeech) source data or Dutch input, and does not natively emit RTTM files - it references only LibriSpeech + WHAM. Building 'DutchMix' requires adapting metadata CSVs, an inference not directly supported out of the box. SparseLibriMix (popcornell/SparseLibriMix) was not independently fetched this pass. Capability plausible but the 'Dutch single-speaker -> exact RTTM' path is not turnkey.

verdicts.claim: JASMIN-CGN is available for academic research via the Dutch-Flemish HLT Agency/INT under a license, not an open one-click download.
verdicts.evidence: https://github.com/syfengcuhk/jasmin README: the repo provides only benchmarking code, not the corpus; 'The Jasmin corpus ... is available for academic research. Please contact the developers for the access to this corpus.' Requires obtaining JASMIN separately (via corpus developers / INT), consistent with a licensed, non-one-click distribution. The referenced Whisper fine-tuning paper (arXiv 2502.17284) context is plausible but the access-model claim is the load-bearing one and is confirmed.

verdicts.claim: N-Best 2008 Dutch benchmark has no open self-service download and is distributed through research/ELRA-style channels built on CGN material.
verdicts.evidence: Springer chapter (10.1007/978-3-642-30910-6_15) and related N-Best papers: benchmark covering Northern+Southern Dutch, broadcast news + conversational telephone speech; 'Resources were shared with the research community through the European Language Resources Association (ELRA).' No public Hugging Face / wget mirror surfaced in search; distribution is via ELRA/research channels. Consistent with 'not downloadable today via self-service'.
